package main

import (
	"log"
	"net/http"

	"github.com/gin-gonic/gin"
	"gorm.io/gorm"
)

// Positive scenario for cmd_or_main_contains_domain_rules.
// Rule intent: main or cmd packages that contain business-rule branches instead of wiring and startup concerns.
// Anti-pattern: Business logic (discount rules, approval thresholds) embedded in main.go.

type Order struct {
	ID       uint    `gorm:"primaryKey"`
	Total    float64 `gorm:"column:total"`
	Status   string  `gorm:"column:status"`
	Customer string  `gorm:"column:customer"`
}

func ApplyDiscount(order *Order) {
	if order.Total > 1000 {
		order.Total *= 0.9
		log.Printf("applied 10%% bulk discount, new total: %.2f", order.Total)
	}
	if order.Total > 5000 {
		order.Total *= 0.95
		log.Printf("applied additional 5%% VIP discount, new total: %.2f", order.Total)
	}
}

func DetermineApprovalLevel(order *Order) string {
	if order.Total < 100 {
		return "auto-approved"
	}
	if order.Total < 1000 {
		return "manager-approval"
	}
	if order.Total < 10000 {
		return "director-approval"
	}
	return "executive-approval"
}

func main() {
	r := gin.Default()

	r.POST("/orders", func(c *gin.Context, db *gorm.DB) {
		var order Order
		if err := c.ShouldBindJSON(&order); err != nil {
			c.JSON(http.StatusBadRequest, gin.H{"error": err.Error()})
			return
		}

		// Domain rules in main package instead of a service layer
		ApplyDiscount(&order)
		order.Status = DetermineApprovalLevel(&order)

		db.Create(&order)
		c.JSON(http.StatusCreated, order)
	})

	r.Run(":8080")
}
